text: The DaVinci Intuitive robotic system allows us to operate doing hernia surgery. It's particularly useful, I think, in colorectal cancer.

We start just the same way as we would for any laparoscopic or open surgery. You're scrubbed, draped, and then as we do for laparoscopic surgery, we have to place the robotic arms and the ports, we call them tiny camera holes or incision sites or trocars, and we put it under direct vision. by using the camera and looking at the screens.
The robotic arms are docked and connected to the patient after placing the ports, followed by the selection and attachment of appropriate surgical instruments to the DaVinci system.

After then I scrub out and I go to robotic console where I sit and look into a viewfinder that's connected to two sort of arms that can hold my fingers and what I do with my fingers the robot mimics in the patient. And I think it's very precise. Not only can you see things magnified, but I can adjust the magnification. I can adjust colors. I can see in three-D.

I have certain dyes that are available to me that show me a good blood supply or structures in the body that you can't see just laparoscopically. With the robot, we can have you stay overnight and go home the next day and really have minimal pain. And I think that's really the beauty of it. And you're looking at, you know, anywhere from three to six tiny incisions depending on what you're having done.
